title: Unitary, Symplectic, and Orthogonal Moments of Moments
speaker: Emma Bailey
abstract: The study of random matrix moments of moments has connections to number theory, combinatorics, and log-correlated fields. Our results give the leading order of these functions for integer moments parameters by exploiting connections with Gelfand-Tsetlin patterns and counts of lattice points in convex sets. This is joint work with Jon Keating and Theo Assiotis.

proof_stages.label: Classical compact groups and one-point characteristic-polynomial moments
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Haar-random unitary, symplectic, and special orthogonal matrices, notes that their eigenvalues lie on the unit circle, and recalls the Keating-Snaith formula and asymptotic polynomial structure for ordinary characteristic-polynomial moments.

proof_stages.label: Symmetry-type motivation from L-functions
proof_stages.summary: Using the Katz-Sarnak/Montgomery-Dyson philosophy, the speaker explains that zeta, quadratic Dirichlet L-functions, and elliptic-curve twist families correspond respectively to unitary, symplectic, and orthogonal random-matrix symmetry types, so random-matrix moment constants are expected to predict arithmetic moment constants.

proof_stages.label: Definition and phase transition for moments of moments
proof_stages.summary: The moment of moments is defined by first averaging a fixed characteristic polynomial around the unit circle and then taking a Haar average over matrices, after which the Fyodorov-Keating unitary conjecture is stated with its subcritical and supercritical regimes and interpreted via Fisher-Hartwig coalescence, statistical-mechanics freezing, and maxima of log-characteristic polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Integer-parameter theorems in the three symmetry classes
proof_stages.summary: For integer k and β, the speaker states the joint results: the unitary moment of moments has the conjectured leading power and is a polynomial in the matrix size, while analogous symplectic and orthogonal formulas have distinct leading exponents, constants, polynomiality, and one small special-orthogonal exception.

proof_stages.label: Three proof routes after exchanging integrals
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses integrality to exchange the circle and matrix averages, then interprets the resulting matrix average through multiple contour integrals, representation-theoretic/Gelfand-Tsetlin descriptions, or Toeplitz/Hankel Fisher-Hartwig asymptotics, with the unitary theorem obtained by the contour route and the symplectic/orthogonal results by the combinatorial representation route.

proof_stages.label: Gelfand-Tsetlin pattern dictionary for symplectic and orthogonal moments
proof_stages.summary: Signatures, interlacing, half patterns, and symplectic versus orthogonal Gelfand-Tsetlin patterns are defined, and a Bump-Gamburd-based proposition identifies the symplectic moment of moments with the number of constrained patterns having a rectangular top row and additional row constraints.

proof_stages.label: Lattice-point asymptotics and resulting structure
proof_stages.summary: After illustrating the constrained-pattern count in the smallest case, the general proof moves from the discrete pattern model to continuous chambers and convex regions, extracts the leading power of n via lattice-point/volume asymptotics, proves positivity of the leading coefficient, and points to structured small polynomials and corresponding L-function conjectures.

examples.name: The k=1 reduction to the Keating-Snaith moment
examples.note: Setting the outer moment parameter k equal to 1 is a routine special case showing that moments of moments recover the classical characteristic-polynomial moment.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks the k=1 specialization by noting it reduces to the Keating-Snaith moment and gives the n^{beta^2} behavior

passes.reason: The case k=1 is a collapsed base case of the moment-of-moments construction, reducing to the ordinary Keating-Snaith moment.
